title: Anurita Srivastava
text:
About
    Anurita Srivastava
    Policy Strategist · Program Manager · Diplomacy
  




  
    
    
      
        I work at the intersection of evidence, strategy, and implementation — translating research into programs, partnerships, and policy decisions that improve how institutions serve people and communities.
      
      
        During the last seven years, I have worked on AI and product apps, public health issues, evaluated social safety programs, developed a pipeline of donations and partnerships for animal welfare NGOs, managed teams of specialists across different timezones, co-started a sustainable clothing brand, supported policy research, and ensured that my findings informed evidence-based decision-making.
      
      
        In graduate school, I enhanced this kind of experience with studies of program evaluation, causality analysis, data analytics, and evidence-based policy-making at Georgetown University. It is vital to ensure that policies aren't only effective in theory; they must be implemented and yield desired effects, which requires not only methodological but also implementation skills.
      
      
      
      As the late Jane Goodall reminds us, “What you do makes a difference, and you have to decide what kind of difference you want to make.” That idea anchors my passion that sits between research and action: evaluating what works, designing programs that can scale responsibly, and helping public and nonprofit institutions make better decisions.

Evaluated global interventions to provide cost-effective frameworks and strategic partnership recommendations.

    Orchestrated a catalytic fundraising strategy that secured a 25% growth in the donor pipeline and a 20% increase in philanthropic revenue within three months. Executed corporate outreach programs that boosted organizational visibility and engagement by 40%.

    Managed a team of 40; streamlined data architecture, boosting response speeds by 48% to facilitate real-time executive decision-making. Authored technical notes and led stakeholder meetings on Key Performance Indicators (KPI) and team output; managed complex Objectives and Key Results (OKRs) to ensure alignment between technical execution and business goals.
  



  
  
    Project Manager - Strategy
    Walking Tree Technologies | Berlin, Germany | Hybrid
    Jan 2023 - Dec 2023
    Managed a diverse team of 20 to deliver end-to-end Software-as-a-service (Saas) projects for clients across the globe, overseeing complex system migrations and ensuring operational agility. Automated monitoring systems and data pipelines, reducing manual reporting by 10 hours/week. Leveraged financial modeling and SWOT to deliver actionable insights, accelerating project deliveries and driving a 28% revenue increase.
  



  
  
    Product Engineer
    Atlassian, Zomato, Wysa | India
    Aug 2019 - Nov 2022
    
      
       Competitively selected for Apple's Entrepreneur Camp at Cupertino, CA in 2019. Represented Wysa with the CEO.
        Accepted the pre-placement offer at Wysa, Promoted twice at Zomato.
        Developed and shipped the IP-patented ‘Smart AI Replies’ feature at Atlassian, utilizing on-device machine learning to enhance user engagement and system efficiency.
        Led the end-to-end incubation of Contactless Dining in 2020, from ideation to final software release, at Zomato. Was a volunteer program officer for their Feeding India NGO.
        Increased premium user conversion by adding accessibility features, better conversion pipeline and Augmented Reality to Wysa's AI mental health chatbot app.

title: Why most program evaluations fail before they start
text: Most program evaluations are set up to fail — not because of bad data collection or poor analysis, but because the fundamental question the evaluation is supposed to answer was never made explicit before the program launched.

title: Why most program evaluations fail before they start
text:
The design problem
When a program goes live before evaluation questions are specified, you end up reverse-engineering a theory of change from whatever data happens to have been collected. This is a bit like trying to navigate by looking at where you’ve already been.
The fix isn’t complicated in principle: specify your logic model before implementation. What inputs go in? What activities happen? What outputs do you expect? What outcomes? Over what timeframe?
But in practice, the political pressures to show results and start delivering almost always crowd out the time needed to do this work carefully.

title: Why most program evaluations fail before they start
text:
What actually helps
From field experience across federal and state program evaluations, the most predictive factor of a successful evaluation isn’t the analyst — it’s whether the program team had a documented theory of change at baseline.
Three things that consistently work:

Embed the evaluator in the design phase, not the post-implementation phase. An evaluator who joins after launch is a historian, not a scientist.
Specify counterfactuals explicitly. What would have happened without this program? If you can’t answer that, you can’t measure impact.
Separate monitoring from evaluation. Tracking outputs (meals served, trainings conducted) is monitoring. Measuring outcomes (food security, employment retention) is evaluation. Conflating them is how programs look successful on paper while failing their beneficiaries.

The methodological sophistication comes later — in the regression model, the difference-in-differences, the matching strategy. But none of that matters if the foundation isn’t right.

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
text:
Fuzzy matching
  ArcGIS · spmap
  Causal inference
  Choropleth mapping

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
section: Part 1: Geospatial fuzzy matching — Tanzania ward redistricting (2010–2015)
text:
Part 1: Geospatial fuzzy matching — Tanzania ward redistricting (2010–2015)
Tanzania’s ward count grew from 3,333 to 3,944 between 2010 and 2015 through administrative subdivision. Matching electoral datasets across this boundary change required reconciling two non-overlapping geographic classifications — a standard problem in any longitudinal sub-national policy analysis.
Method: Used ArcGIS shapefile intersection data (2012 and 2017 shapefiles) to compute the percentage area overlap between each 2015 ward and all candidate 2010 parent wards. Applied a 50% area-overlap threshold to classify parent-child relationships.

  
    2,705
    Stable wards (unchanged 2010–2015)
  
  
    488
    Split into exactly 2 new wards
  
  
    45
    Split into 3 or more wards
  
  
    440 / 616
    Parentless / orphan wards
  

Top regions by ward division rate:



Region
Division rate
Context




Rukwa
39.7%
Highest — major administrative restructuring


Morogoro
34.7%



Katavi
28.6%



Mtwara
28.5%



Arusha
25.6%



Kagera
5.0%
Lowest — minimal redistricting



Policy relevance: This kind of boundary reconciliation is essential for any longitudinal analysis of sub-national government data — elections, health outcomes, census linkages, or fiscal transfers.

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
section: Part 2: Debiasing treatment effect estimates — Monte Carlo simulation
text:
Part 2: Debiasing treatment effect estimates — Monte Carlo simulation
Designed a DGP with a known treatment effect (β = 0.3 SD), then built 5 regression models varying which controls were included — confounder, mediator, and collider — running 200 simulations at each of 6 sample sizes.
Key finding: Only Model 2 (controlling for the confounder alone) recovers β = 0.30. A larger sample size cannot fix structural bias — Models 1 and 5 remain wrong at N=2,500.



Model
Controls included
Bias direction
Recovers β = 0.30?




M1: Naive
None
Upward (~0.57)
❌


M2: Correct
Confounder only
None
✅


M3: + Mediator
Confounder + mediator
None (higher variance)
✅


M4: Collider
Confounder + collider
Downward (~0.15)
❌


M5: All
All covariates
Fully attenuated (~0.04)
❌



Collider warning: Including a variable caused by both treatment and outcome introduces downward bias of ~0.15 — a counterintuitive and commonly overlooked error in applied policy research that gets worse with more data.

title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
section: Part 3: Spatial analysis — U.S. animal welfare enforcement choropleth
text:
Part 3: Spatial analysis — U.S. animal welfare enforcement choropleth
Merged a hand-coded Animal Welfare Penalty Harshness Index (0–100 scale, 48 states) with U.S. Census shapefiles in Stata using spmap, producing a choropleth revealing stark regional disparities in enforcement.
Method: shp2dta to import Census shapefiles → merge with penalty index → spmap with quantile color breaks → explicit handling of missing data (WY, SD) shown in legend as gray.
Finding: Northeast and Pacific Coast states score 75+; the Deep South and Mountain West cluster below 35 — consistent with broader regulatory divergence patterns in U.S. federalism.

  Skills demonstrated
  
    Geospatial fuzzy matching
    Shapefile processing
    Panel data reconciliation
    Stata bysort / merge
    DAG reasoning
    Collider / confounder identification
    Choropleth mapping
    ArcGIS · spmap

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
text:
Stata · simulate
  Monte Carlo methods
  Power analysis
  Cluster RCT design

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
section: Part 1: Sampling noise in a fixed population
text:
Part 1: Sampling noise in a fixed population
Generated a fixed population of 10,000 individuals with a true Y–X relationship (seed-fixed for reproducibility), then ran 500 regression simulations at four sample sizes to quantify how estimation precision changes as we draw larger subsamples from a bounded population.
Key finding: At N=10, beta estimates vary wildly across simulations despite being unbiased on average. At N=10,000 (the full population), all sampling variance disappears entirely — this is the finite-population ceiling.



Sample size (N)
Mean β̂
Avg. SEM
95% CI width
% significant




10
≈ true β
High
Very wide
~30–40%


100
≈ true β
Moderate
Wide
~70–80%


1,000
≈ true β
Small
Narrow
~98%+


10,000
= true β
Near zero
Minimal
100%

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
section: Part 2: Sampling noise in an infinite superpopulation
text:
Part 2: Sampling noise in an infinite superpopulation
Extended the simulation to an infinite superpopulation DGP — generating fresh data each run — across 26 sample sizes: all 20 powers of 2 (4 → 524,288) plus N = 10, 100, 1,000, 10,000, 100,000, and 1,000,000.
Why superpopulation allows N = 1,000,000: In Part 1 the population is capped at 10,000. The superpopulation DGP draws from an infinite distribution each run — sample size is unbounded.
SEM difference: In the superpopulation, variance shrinks as 1/√N but never hits zero. At the same N, superpopulation SEM is slightly larger than fixed-population SEM, because the fixed population’s known finite structure removes a layer of uncertainty.

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
text:
Part 3: Individual-level power calculations
Designed a DGP where Y ~ N(0,1), treatment effect = 0.1 SD (uniform 0.0–0.2 SD), 50/50 treatment-control split. Calculated minimum sample sizes for 80% power under three scenarios:

  
    ~3,142
    Baseline N (80% power, 0.1 SD effect)
  
  
    ~3,697
    After 15% attrition (+18% larger)
  
  
    ~4,190
    30% treatment arm (unequal split)
  

Attrition: Losing 15% of participants symmetrically inflates required sample by ~18% — you must recruit enough to still have sufficient power after dropout.
Unequal arms: Shifting to 30/70 treatment-control requires a larger total N. The smaller arm is the binding constraint on precision. The 50/50 split is always the most efficient design for a fixed total N.

title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
text:
Part 4: Cluster RCT design — ICC = 0.3
Designed a school-based intervention where treatment is assigned at the school level — not student level. Treatment effect = 0.2 SD (uniform 0.15–0.25 SD), ICC/ρ ≈ 0.3.
Design effect at 15 students/school: DEFF = 1 + (15–1) × 0.3 = 5.2 — meaning you need roughly 5× as many observations as an individual-level RCT.
Power plateaus quickly: With 200 schools fixed, power reaches ~80% around 8–16 students/school. Beyond that, adding students yields near-zero gains due to high ICC.








Scenario
Schools needed
Total students




Full compliance (100% uptake)
~100 schools
~1,500


70% adoption (partial compliance)
~145 schools
~2,175



Partial compliance finding: When only 70% of assigned schools implement the intervention, the required school count rises by ~45%. Non-compliance is a design problem, not just a fidelity problem.

  Skills demonstrated
  
    Monte Carlo simulation
    Stata simulate command
    RCT design
    Power analysis
    Cluster randomization
    ICC / design effect
    Attrition modeling
    Reproducible code
